1 # Recipe Bottle 2 3 > [!IMPORTANT] 4 > **Early-stage project — security review welcome** 5 > 6 > Recipe Bottle runs untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) — a three-container security apparatus where a privileged [Pentacle](#Pentacle) establishes the network namespace, a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces iptables policy on dual networks, and the [Bottle](#Bottle) workload has no direct network access. The supply chain is hardened with SLSA provenance verification, least-privilege service accounts, and no secrets in version control. 7 > 8 > This architecture is deliberate, but it has not yet had broad independent review — particularly the runtime containment (iptables rules, privileged namespace setup, network isolation enforcement). If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. 9 10 Recipe Bottle helps you build container images with rigorous supply-chain provenance, and run untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) that enforces network isolation without touching the workload. A developer can adopt either domain alone; the two are designed to compose, not to depend on each other. 11 12 Recipe Bottle is a set of bash scripts designed to be incorporated into arbitrary projects — via `git subtree`, `git subrepo`, `git submodule`, or simply by copying a few directories into place. The workstation floor is deliberately narrow: `bash 3.2`, `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq`, `openssl`, and `docker`. There is no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, no `gcloud` CLI on the workstation — Google Cloud operations run as REST calls over `curl` and `jq`. A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. 13 14 **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle 15 16

17 18

19 20 ## Supply Chain 21 22 Recipe Bottle orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with SLSA attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. Builds run in an egress-locked configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability. 23 24 ### Vessel 25

file.content:
# Recipe Bottle > [!IMPORTANT] > **Early-stage project — security review welcome** > > Recipe Bottle runs untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) — a three-container security apparatus where a privileged [Pentacle](#Pentacle) establishes the network namespace, a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces iptables policy on dual networks, and the [Bottle](#Bottle) workload has no direct network access. The supply chain is hardened with SLSA provenance verification, least-privilege service accounts, and no secrets in version control. > > This architecture is deliberate, but it has not yet had broad independent review — particularly the runtime containment (iptables rules, privileged namespace setup, network isolation enforcement). If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. Recipe Bottle helps you build container images with rigorous supply-chain provenance, and run untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) that enforces network isolation without touching the workload. A developer can adopt either domain alone; the two are designed to compose, not to depend on each other. Recipe Bottle is a set of bash scripts designed to be incorporated into arbitrary projects — via `git subtree`, `git subrepo`, `git submodule`, or simply by copying a few directories into place. The workstation floor is deliberately narrow: `bash 3.2`, `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq`, `openssl`, and `docker`. There is no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, no `gcloud` CLI on the workstation — Google Cloud operations run as REST calls over `curl` and `jq`. A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle

## Supply Chain Recipe Bottle orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with SLSA attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. Builds run in an egress-locked configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability. ### Vessel

content.content:
1 # Recipe Bottle 2 3 > [!IMPORTANT] 4 > **Early-stage project — security review welcome** 5 > 6 > Recipe Bottle runs untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) — a three-container security apparatus where a privileged [Pentacle](#Pentacle) establishes the network namespace, a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces iptables policy on dual networks, and the [Bottle](#Bottle) workload has no direct network access. The supply chain is hardened with SLSA provenance verification, least-privilege service accounts, and no secrets in version control. 7 > 8 > This architecture is deliberate, but it has not yet had broad independent review — particularly the runtime containment (iptables rules, privileged namespace setup, network isolation enforcement). If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. 9 10 Recipe Bottle helps you build container images with rigorous supply-chain provenance, and run untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) that enforces network isolation without touching the workload. A developer can adopt either domain alone; the two are designed to compose, not to depend on each other. 11 12 Recipe Bottle is a set of bash scripts designed to be incorporated into arbitrary projects — via `git subtree`, `git subrepo`, `git submodule`, or simply by copying a few directories into place. The workstation floor is deliberately narrow: `bash 3.2`, `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq`, `openssl`, and `docker`. There is no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, no `gcloud` CLI on the workstation — Google Cloud operations run as REST calls over `curl` and `jq`. A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. 13 14 **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle 15 16

17 18

19 20 ## Supply Chain 21 22 Recipe Bottle orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with SLSA attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. Builds run in an egress-locked configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability. 23 24 ### Vessel 25 26 A specification for a container image — built from source ([Conjure](#Conjure)), mirrored from upstream ([Bind](#Bind)), or pushed from local ([Graft](#Graft)). Each [Vessel](#Vessel) is a directory under `rbev-vessels/` containing at minimum an `rbrv.env` configuration file; [Conjure](#Conjure) [Vessels](#Vessel) also include a Dockerfile. The [Vessel](#Vessel) mode determines how the [Director](#Director) [Ordains](#Ordain) its [Hallmark](#Hallmark). 27 28 ### Hallmark 29 30 A specific build instance of a [Vessel](#Vessel), identified by timestamp. [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) are the unit of provenance tracking — each one records when and how the image was produced. [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) regime files to pin a [Crucible](#Crucible) to specific image versions. 31 32 ### Vouch 33 34 Cryptographic attestation proving a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) was built by trusted infrastructure. The [Vouch](#Vouch) verdict is mode-aware: [Conjure](#Conjure) builds receive full SLSA provenance verification, [Bind](#Bind) builds receive digest-pin verification, and [Graft](#Graft) builds receive a GRAFTED verdict with no provenance chain. The [Director](#Director) [Vouches](#Vouch) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) after [Tallying](#Tally) their build status. 35 36 ### Depot 37 38 The facility where container images are built and stored — a GCP project with an artifact registry and a storage bucket. The [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), and the [Governor](#Governor) administers access to it. Each [Depot](#Depot) operates as an independent supply-chain boundary with its own credentials, builds, and registry. 39 40 ### Ordain 41 42 Create a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) with full attestation — the production build operation. [Ordaining](#Ordain) is mode-aware: it [Conjures](#Conjure), [Binds](#Bind), or [Grafts](#Graft) depending on the [Vessel's](#Vessel) configuration. Each [Ordain](#Ordain) produces an image in the registry with associated provenance metadata. 43 44 ### Conjure 45 46 [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: Cloud Build creates the image from source. [Conjure](#Conjure) builds run in an egress-locked environment with digest-pinned toolchains, producing full SLSA attestation and SBOMs. This is the highest-trust build mode. 47 48 ### Bind 49 50 [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: mirror an upstream image pinned by digest. [Binding](#Bind) captures an external image at a specific digest into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. Trust is established through digest-pin verification rather than build provenance. 51 52 ### Graft 53 54 [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: push a locally-built image to the registry. [Grafting](#Graft) uploads a local image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image itself, though about and [Vouch](#Vouch) metadata still run in Cloud Build. This is the lowest-trust mode (GRAFTED verdict). 55 56 ### Kludge 57 58 Build a [Vessel](#Vessel) image locally for fast iteration, without registry push. [Kludging](#Kludge) produces a local Docker image for development and testing without involving Cloud Build or the [Depot](#Depot). The resulting image can be used to [Charge](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) directly. 59 60 ### Enshrine 61 62 Mirror upstream base images into your [Depot's](#Depot) registry. [Enshrining](#Enshrine) ensures the build pipeline has a fixed, self-contained supply chain — builds draw from project-owned copies rather than depending on third-party registry availability at build time. 63 64 ### Summon 65 66 Pull a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) image from the [Depot](#Depot) to your local machine. The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images for local use — the final step before a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) can be used in a [Crucible](#Crucible). 67 68 ### Plumb 69 70 Inspect an artifact's provenance — SBOM, build info, and [Vouch](#Vouch) chain. [Plumbing](#Plumb) provides full transparency into how an image was built and what it contains. Two views are available: full (SBOM, build info, Dockerfile) and compact (attestation summary). 71 72 ### Tally 73 74 Inventory [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) in the registry by health status. [Tallying](#Tally) shows which builds succeeded, which are pending, and which failed. The [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) before [Vouching](#Vouch) to confirm build completion. 75 76 ### Levy 77 78 Provision a new [Depot's](#Depot) GCP infrastructure. [Levying](#Levy) creates the GCP project, artifact registry, storage bucket, and build configuration. This is a [Payor](#Payor) operation that binds regime configuration to real cloud resources. 79 80 ### Payor 81 82 Owns the GCP project and funds it; authenticates via OAuth. The [Payor](#Payor) is the only role requiring manual Google Cloud Console interaction — establishing the project, configuring OAuth, and authorizing via browser flow. All other roles descend from credentials the [Payor's](#Payor) infrastructure creates. 83 84 ### Governor 85 86 Administers a [Depot](#Depot): creates service accounts, manages access. The [Governor](#Governor) is mantled by the [Payor](#Payor) and holds the administrative credential for the [Depot](#Depot). The [Governor](#Governor) [Knights](#Knight) [Directors](#Director) and [Charters](#Charter) [Retrievers](#Retriever). 87 88 ### Director 89 90 Builds and publishes [Vessel](#Vessel) images into a [Depot](#Depot). The [Director](#Director) [Ordains](#Ordain) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark), [Tallies](#Tally) build status, [Vouches](#Vouch) provenance, and manages the image lifecycle. Each [Director](#Director) credential is scoped to one [Depot](#Depot). 91 92 ### Retriever 93 94 Pulls and runs [Vessel](#Vessel) images from a [Depot](#Depot). The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images and [Plumbs](#Plumb) their provenance. This is the most constrained role — read-only access to the registry. 95 96 ### Charter 97 98 Create a [Retriever](#Retriever) service account — a [Governor](#Governor) operation. [Chartering](#Charter) provisions a new credential scoped to image pull access within a single [Depot](#Depot). 99 100 ### Knight 101 102 Create a [Director](#Director) service account — a [Governor](#Governor) operation. [Knighting](#Knight) provisions a new credential scoped to build and publish access within a single [Depot](#Depot). 103 104 ## Crucible 105 106 The distinctive case Recipe Bottle addresses is *running untrusted code*: third-party tooling, experimental packages, binaries with uncertain provenance. Containers excel at packaging known applications, but running unvetted code poses security risks that ordinary container deployment does not solve. Recipe Bottle assembles a [Crucible](#Crucible) — three cooperating containers where a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces network policy via `iptables` and `dnsmasq` — without requiring modifications to existing container images. The [Bottle](#Bottle) container runs unmodified, in a network namespace prepared by a privileged [Pentacle](#Pentacle), with all egress flowing through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway. 107 108 ### Nameplate 109 110 Per-[Vessel](#Vessel) configuration tying a [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) together into a runnable [Crucible](#Crucible). The [Nameplate](#Nameplate) moniker (e.g. `tadmor`) identifies the unit across all operations. Each [Nameplate](#Nameplate) declares its [Vessel](#Vessel) selections, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) pins, and the network policy that the [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces. 111 112 ### Sentry 113 114 Security container enforcing network policies via `iptables` and `dnsmasq`. The [Sentry](#Sentry) applies two layers of egress policy: DNS-level filtering (only allowed domain names resolve) and IP-level filtering (only allowed CIDR ranges pass). A compromised [Bottle](#Bottle) cannot bypass either layer — the [Sentry](#Sentry) is the sole gateway between the [Bottle](#Bottle) and the outside network. 115 116 ### Pentacle 117 118 Privileged container establishing the network namespace shared with the [Bottle](#Bottle). The [Pentacle](#Pentacle) runs briefly with elevated privileges to create the network topology, then remains as the namespace anchor. Security policies are enforced from the first packet because the [Sentry](#Sentry) configures the namespace before the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts. 119 120 ### Bottle 121 122 Your workload container, running unmodified in a controlled network environment. The [Bottle](#Bottle) has no direct network access — all traffic routes through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway in a namespace prepared by the [Pentacle](#Pentacle). Any existing container image can run as a [Bottle](#Bottle) without modification. 123 124 ### Crucible 125 126 The [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad running together as one unit for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). The [Crucible](#Crucible) is the local safety orchestration — the apparatus that makes running untrusted code practical. [Charging](#Charge) starts all three containers; [Quenching](#Quench) stops and cleans them up. 127 128 ### Charge 129 130 Start the [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). [Charging](#Charge) brings up the [Crucible](#Crucible) in dependency order: [Pentacle](#Pentacle) creates the namespace, [Sentry](#Sentry) configures policy, then the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts with its network already constrained. 131 132 ### Quench 133 134 Stop and clean up a [Charged](#Charge) [Nameplate's](#Nameplate) containers. [Quenching](#Quench) tears down the [Crucible](#Crucible) in reverse order and removes the network resources created during [Charging](#Charge). 135 136 ## How It Works 137 138 Recipe Bottle addresses two orthogonal complexity domains: building container images with verifiable provenance, and running untrusted images with enforced network isolation. The two tracks compose but neither requires the other. 139 140 ### Image Management 141 142 Recipe Bottle builds container images on Google Cloud Build (GCB) and stores them in Google Artifact Registry (GAR): 143 144 - Isolated build environments using Google-curated Cloud Build builder images 145 - Multi-architecture support via `docker buildx` with binfmt emulation 146 - SLSA provenance attestation and verification 147 - Software Bills of Material (SBOM) for every build 148 - Full build transcripts captured as auxiliary metadata artifacts 149 - Upstream base images [Enshrined](#Enshrine) into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry, so builds do not depend on third-party registry availability at build time 150 - `gcloud` never runs on the workstation — REST calls via `curl` and `jq` drive all remote operations, and the Google-supplied `gcloud` binary is confined to Cloud Build step containers on the server side 151 152 ### Crucible Orchestration 153 154 For running containers with network services, Recipe Bottle orchestrates three containers working together: 155 156 - **[Sentry](#Sentry)** — enforces network security policies via `iptables` and `dnsmasq` 157 - **[Pentacle](#Pentacle)** — establishes a privileged network namespace and shares it with the [Bottle](#Bottle) 158 - **[Bottle](#Bottle)** — your workload container, running unmodified in a controlled network environment 159 160 This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the [Bottle](#Bottle) container experiences only a functional path to its [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway. 161 162 The [Sentry](#Sentry) applies two layers of egress policy: `dnsmasq` answers DNS queries only for explicitly allowed names, and `iptables` permits outbound IP traffic only to allowed CIDR ranges. The two layers combine into an enforced allowlist that a compromised or misbehaving [Bottle](#Bottle) cannot bypass — neither by DNS-based exfiltration nor by direct IP connection to an unapproved destination. Each [Crucible's](#Crucible) allowlist is declared in a [Nameplate](#Nameplate) regime file alongside the [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) [Vessel](#Vessel) selections, making network policy a reviewable artifact of the configuration rather than an implicit runtime behavior. 163 164 ### Project Direction 165 166 **Build pipeline.** Egress lockdown is implemented via a dual-pool Cloud Build architecture. VPC Service Controls and cosign signing are evaluated and deferred until organizational policy or external distribution triggers them. 167 168 **Runtime.** Docker on Linux is the first-class runtime; rootless Podman and macOS-native workflows are deferred pending user demand. One known weakness: when allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse — DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN ranges. 169 170 **Networking.** [Bottle](#Bottle)-to-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication is feasible under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model but not implemented; waiting for a concrete use case. 171 172 ## Prerequisites 173 174 - macOS or Linux workstation 175 - `bash` (3.2+), `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq`, `openssl` 176 - `docker` (container runtime) 177 - A Google Cloud account with billing enabled (credit card required for verification; free tier is sufficient to start) 178 179 ## Using the CLI 180 181 All Recipe Bottle operations are invoked through lightweight shell scripts in the `tt/` directory, run from the project root. Tab completion narrows by prefix. The project's `CLAUDE.md` provides a complete command reference; the CLI's interactive walkthroughs are the authoritative procedure source. 182 183 ## Setup 184 185 Recipe Bottle uses a role-based security model with four roles, each building on the previous: 186 187 | Role | Authenticates via | Purpose | 188 |------|-------------------|---------| 189 | [**Payor**](#Payor) | OAuth (browser flow) | Creates/funds GCP infrastructure, manages [Governor](#Governor) lifecycle | 190 | [**Governor**](#Governor) | Service account credential | Administers [Director](#Director) and [Retriever](#Retriever) credentials within a [Depot](#Depot) | 191 | [**Director**](#Director) | Service account credential | Submits builds, manages images, verifies provenance | 192 | [**Retriever**](#Retriever) | Service account credential | Pulls images for local use | 193 194 The [Payor](#Payor) stands apart — it requires manual Google Cloud Console work and OAuth authentication. All downstream roles authenticate via credential files, enabling full automation. 195 196 ### Establishment and Provisioning 197 198 The [Payor](#Payor) begins by creating a GCP project and configuring an OAuth consent screen through the Google Cloud Console. After downloading the OAuth client credentials, the [Payor](#Payor) installs them via a browser authorization flow — the resulting refresh token is stored locally with restrictive permissions and does not need to be repeated. 199 200 With [Payor](#Payor) credentials in place, the [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), provisioning it with build infrastructure, artifact registry, and secrets storage. The [Payor](#Payor) then mantles a [Governor](#Governor) service account to administer the [Depot](#Depot). 201 202 ### Credential Distribution 203 204 The [Governor](#Governor) creates downstream credentials: [Knighting](#Knight) a [Director](#Director) for build operations and [Chartering](#Charter) a [Retriever](#Retriever) for image pull access. Each credential is scoped to a single role within a single [Depot](#Depot). 205 206 ### Build and Retrieve 207 208 The [Director](#Director) [Ordains](#Ordain) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) for each [Vessel](#Vessel) — [Conjuring](#Conjure) from source, [Binding](#Bind) from upstream, or [Grafting](#Graft) from local builds. After builds complete, the [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) by health status and [Vouches](#Vouch) their provenance. [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values from the [Tally](#Tally) are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) regime files, completing the chain from build to runtime. 209 210 The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images locally for use. 211 212 ## Day-to-Day Operations 213 214 To run a workload, [Charge](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). This starts the [Sentry](#Sentry), [Pentacle](#Pentacle), and [Bottle](#Bottle) containers together — the [Bottle](#Bottle) is ready for interactive use immediately. 215 216 For diagnostics, shell into the [Bottle](#Bottle) or the [Sentry](#Sentry), or observe network traffic across the [Crucible's](#Crucible) containers. When finished, [Quench](#Quench) the [Crucible](#Crucible) to stop and clean up all three containers. 217 218 To inspect an image's supply chain, [Plumb](#Plumb) its provenance — the full view shows the SBOM, build info, and Dockerfile; the compact view summarizes the attestation chain. 219 220 ## Credential Safety 221 222 All credential files require `600` permissions and must never be committed to version control. 223 224 | Credential | Location | Created during | 225 |------------|----------|----------------| 226 | [Payor](#Payor) OAuth | `~/.rbw/rbro.env` | [Payor](#Payor) installation | 227 | [Governor](#Governor) | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/governor/rbra.env` | [Governor](#Governor) mantling | 228 | [Director](#Director) | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/director/rbra.env` | [Director](#Director) [Knighting](#Knight) | 229 | [Retriever](#Retriever) | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/retriever/rbra.env` | [Retriever](#Retriever) [Chartering](#Charter) | 230 231 Each credential file is scoped to one role within one [Depot](#Depot) and cannot operate outside its designation. 232 233 ## Configuration 234 235 Recipe Bottle uses a Config Regime system — structured configuration with typed validation. Each regime has a render command (display current values) and a validate command (check correctness). 236 237 ### User-Configured Regimes 238 239 | Regime | File | Purpose | 240 |--------|------|---------| 241 | RBRP | `.rbk/rbrp.env` | [Payor](#Payor) GCP project identity | 242 | RBRR | `.rbk/rbrr.env` | [Depot](#Depot) project, region, build config | 243 | RBRN | `.rbk/*/rbrn.env` | Per-[Vessel](#Vessel): runtime, [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) | 244 | RBRV | [Vessel](#Vessel) dirs | Container image build definitions | 245 | RBRS | station file | Developer machine paths (not in git) | 246 247 ### Managed Regimes (generated by operations) 248 249 | Regime | Purpose | Generated during | 250 |--------|---------|-----------------| 251 | RBRO | OAuth refresh token | [Payor](#Payor) installation | 252 | RBRA | Service account credentials | Mantling, [Knighting](#Knight), [Chartering](#Charter) | 253 254 ### BUK Base Regimes 255 256 | Regime | File | Purpose | 257 |--------|------|---------| 258 | BURC | `.buk/burc.env` | Project structure (tabtarget dir, tools dir) | 259 | BURS | `../station-files/burs.env` | Developer machine (log dir). Not in git. | 260 261 ## Vessels and Nameplates 262 263 **[Vessels](#Vessel)** are build definitions — each is a directory under `rbev-vessels/`: 264 265 ``` 266 rbev-vessels/ 267 ├── rbev-sentry-debian-slim/ # Sentry vessel (conjure — built from source) 268 │ ├── Dockerfile 269 │ └── rbrv.env 270 ├── rbev-bottle-ifrit/ # Ifrit bottle vessel (conjure — built from source) 271 │ ├── Dockerfile 272 │ └── rbrv.env 273 └── rbev-bottle-plantuml/ # PlantUML server (bind — upstream image pinned by digest) 274 └── rbrv.env 275 ``` 276 277 [Conjure](#Conjure) [Vessels](#Vessel) have a Dockerfile and are built by Cloud Build. [Bind](#Bind) [Vessels](#Vessel) (like `rbev-bottle-plantuml`) pin an external image by digest in `rbrv.env` — no Dockerfile, no build step. [Graft](#Graft) [Vessels](#Vessel) push a locally-built image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image, but about and [Vouch](#Vouch) still run in Cloud Build. The same [Ordain](#Ordain) operation handles all three: it detects the [Vessel](#Vessel) mode and triggers a Cloud Build ([Conjure](#Conjure)), mirrors from upstream ([Bind](#Bind)), or pushes a local image ([Graft](#Graft)). Trust hierarchy: [Conjure](#Conjure) has full SLSA provenance, [Bind](#Bind) has digest-pin verification, [Graft](#Graft) has no provenance chain (GRAFTED verdict). 278 279 **[Nameplates](#Nameplate)** tie [Vessels](#Vessel) together into a runnable [Crucible](#Crucible). The [Nameplate](#Nameplate) moniker (e.g. `tadmor`) identifies the unit across all operations: 280 281 ``` 282 .rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env # Maps tadmor → rbev-sentry-debian-slim + rbev-bottle-ifrit 283 ``` 284 285 [Charging](#Charge) `tadmor` starts the [Crucible](#Crucible) defined by the `tadmor` [Nameplate](#Nameplate), which selects its [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) [Vessel](#Vessel) images. 286 287 ## Testing 288 289 Run test fixtures sequentially — they share regime state and container namespaces. Never run fixtures in parallel. 290 291 Two qualification gates exercise the full system: a fast qualify checks tabtarget structure, colophon integrity, and [Nameplate](#Nameplate) health; a release qualify adds shellcheck analysis and the complete test suite. 292 293 ## Recovery 294 295 - **Lost OAuth credentials**: Download a fresh JSON key from Google Cloud Console and re-run the [Payor](#Payor) installation 296 - **Expired tokens**: Run the [Payor](#Payor) refresh operation 297 - **Compromised [Governor](#Governor)**: Re-mantle the [Governor](#Governor) (replaces the service account, invalidates the old credential) 298 - **Compromised [Director](#Director)/[Retriever](#Retriever)**: Forfeit the compromised account, then re-[Knight](#Knight) or re-[Charter](#Charter) 299 - **Lost [Nameplate](#Nameplate) values**: Re-[Tally](#Tally) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) to retrieve values from the registry 300 - **Build timeout or failure**: [Tally](#Tally) to check build status, review logs in the GCP Console for the [Depot](#Depot) project 301 302 ## Architecture 303 304 ``` 305 Project Root/ 306 ├── .buk/ # BUK launcher directory + BURC config 307 ├── .rbk/ # Recipe Bottle config regimes 308 ├── tt/ # TabTargets — all CLI commands live here 309 ├── Tools/ 310 │ ├── buk/ # Bash Utility Kit (portable CLI infrastructure) 311 │ └── rbk/ # Recipe Bottle Kit (domain logic) 312 └── rbev-vessels/ # Vessel definitions (Dockerfile + rbrv.env per vessel) 313 ``` 314 315 ## Claude Code 316 317 If you use [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code), the project includes a `CLAUDE.md` with a full command reference table, glossary, and conventions for AI-assisted development. 318 319 ## License 320 321 Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc. 322 323 Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0. 324

# Recipe Bottle > [!IMPORTANT] > **Early-stage project — security review welcome** > > Recipe Bottle runs untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) — a three-container security apparatus where a privileged [Pentacle](#Pentacle) establishes the network namespace, a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces iptables policy on dual networks, and the [Bottle](#Bottle) workload has no direct network access. The supply chain is hardened with SLSA provenance verification, least-privilege service accounts, and no secrets in version control. > > This architecture is deliberate, but it has not yet had broad independent review — particularly the runtime containment (iptables rules, privileged namespace setup, network isolation enforcement). If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. Recipe Bottle helps you build container images with rigorous supply-chain provenance, and run untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) that enforces network isolation without touching the workload. A developer can adopt either domain alone; the two are designed to compose, not to depend on each other. Recipe Bottle is a set of bash scripts designed to be incorporated into arbitrary projects — via `git subtree`, `git subrepo`, `git submodule`, or simply by copying a few directories into place. The workstation floor is deliberately narrow: `bash 3.2`, `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq`, `openssl`, and `docker`. There is no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, no `gcloud` CLI on the workstation — Google Cloud operations run as REST calls over `curl` and `jq`. A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle

## Supply Chain Recipe Bottle orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with SLSA attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. Builds run in an egress-locked configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability. ### Vessel A specification for a container image — built from source ([Conjure](#Conjure)), mirrored from upstream ([Bind](#Bind)), or pushed from local ([Graft](#Graft)). Each [Vessel](#Vessel) is a directory under `rbev-vessels/` containing at minimum an `rbrv.env` configuration file; [Conjure](#Conjure) [Vessels](#Vessel) also include a Dockerfile. The [Vessel](#Vessel) mode determines how the [Director](#Director) [Ordains](#Ordain) its [Hallmark](#Hallmark). ### Hallmark A specific build instance of a [Vessel](#Vessel), identified by timestamp. [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) are the unit of provenance tracking — each one records when and how the image was produced. [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) regime files to pin a [Crucible](#Crucible) to specific image versions. ### Vouch Cryptographic attestation proving a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) was built by trusted infrastructure. The [Vouch](#Vouch) verdict is mode-aware: [Conjure](#Conjure) builds receive full SLSA provenance verification, [Bind](#Bind) builds receive digest-pin verification, and [Graft](#Graft) builds receive a GRAFTED verdict with no provenance chain. The [Director](#Director) [Vouches](#Vouch) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) after [Tallying](#Tally) their build status. ### Depot The facility where container images are built and stored — a GCP project with an artifact registry and a storage bucket. The [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), and the [Governor](#Governor) administers access to it. Each [Depot](#Depot) operates as an independent supply-chain boundary with its own credentials, builds, and registry. ### Ordain Create a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) with full attestation — the production build operation. [Ordaining](#Ordain) is mode-aware: it [Conjures](#Conjure), [Binds](#Bind), or [Grafts](#Graft) depending on the [Vessel's](#Vessel) configuration. Each [Ordain](#Ordain) produces an image in the registry with associated provenance metadata. ### Conjure [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: Cloud Build creates the image from source. [Conjure](#Conjure) builds run in an egress-locked environment with digest-pinned toolchains, producing full SLSA attestation and SBOMs. This is the highest-trust build mode. ### Bind [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: mirror an upstream image pinned by digest. [Binding](#Bind) captures an external image at a specific digest into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry. Trust is established through digest-pin verification rather than build provenance. ### Graft [Ordain](#Ordain) mode: push a locally-built image to the registry. [Grafting](#Graft) uploads a local image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image itself, though about and [Vouch](#Vouch) metadata still run in Cloud Build. This is the lowest-trust mode (GRAFTED verdict). ### Kludge Build a [Vessel](#Vessel) image locally for fast iteration, without registry push. [Kludging](#Kludge) produces a local Docker image for development and testing without involving Cloud Build or the [Depot](#Depot). The resulting image can be used to [Charge](#Charge) a [Crucible](#Crucible) directly. ### Enshrine Mirror upstream base images into your [Depot's](#Depot) registry. [Enshrining](#Enshrine) ensures the build pipeline has a fixed, self-contained supply chain — builds draw from project-owned copies rather than depending on third-party registry availability at build time. ### Summon Pull a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) image from the [Depot](#Depot) to your local machine. The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images for local use — the final step before a [Hallmark](#Hallmark) can be used in a [Crucible](#Crucible). ### Plumb Inspect an artifact's provenance — SBOM, build info, and [Vouch](#Vouch) chain. [Plumbing](#Plumb) provides full transparency into how an image was built and what it contains. Two views are available: full (SBOM, build info, Dockerfile) and compact (attestation summary). ### Tally Inventory [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) in the registry by health status. [Tallying](#Tally) shows which builds succeeded, which are pending, and which failed. The [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) before [Vouching](#Vouch) to confirm build completion. ### Levy Provision a new [Depot's](#Depot) GCP infrastructure. [Levying](#Levy) creates the GCP project, artifact registry, storage bucket, and build configuration. This is a [Payor](#Payor) operation that binds regime configuration to real cloud resources. ### Payor Owns the GCP project and funds it; authenticates via OAuth. The [Payor](#Payor) is the only role requiring manual Google Cloud Console interaction — establishing the project, configuring OAuth, and authorizing via browser flow. All other roles descend from credentials the [Payor's](#Payor) infrastructure creates. ### Governor Administers a [Depot](#Depot): creates service accounts, manages access. The [Governor](#Governor) is mantled by the [Payor](#Payor) and holds the administrative credential for the [Depot](#Depot). The [Governor](#Governor) [Knights](#Knight) [Directors](#Director) and [Charters](#Charter) [Retrievers](#Retriever). ### Director Builds and publishes [Vessel](#Vessel) images into a [Depot](#Depot). The [Director](#Director) [Ordains](#Ordain) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark), [Tallies](#Tally) build status, [Vouches](#Vouch) provenance, and manages the image lifecycle. Each [Director](#Director) credential is scoped to one [Depot](#Depot). ### Retriever Pulls and runs [Vessel](#Vessel) images from a [Depot](#Depot). The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images and [Plumbs](#Plumb) their provenance. This is the most constrained role — read-only access to the registry. ### Charter Create a [Retriever](#Retriever) service account — a [Governor](#Governor) operation. [Chartering](#Charter) provisions a new credential scoped to image pull access within a single [Depot](#Depot). ### Knight Create a [Director](#Director) service account — a [Governor](#Governor) operation. [Knighting](#Knight) provisions a new credential scoped to build and publish access within a single [Depot](#Depot). ## Crucible The distinctive case Recipe Bottle addresses is *running untrusted code*: third-party tooling, experimental packages, binaries with uncertain provenance. Containers excel at packaging known applications, but running unvetted code poses security risks that ordinary container deployment does not solve. Recipe Bottle assembles a [Crucible](#Crucible) — three cooperating containers where a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces network policy via `iptables` and `dnsmasq` — without requiring modifications to existing container images. The [Bottle](#Bottle) container runs unmodified, in a network namespace prepared by a privileged [Pentacle](#Pentacle), with all egress flowing through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway. ### Nameplate Per-[Vessel](#Vessel) configuration tying a [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) together into a runnable [Crucible](#Crucible). The [Nameplate](#Nameplate) moniker (e.g. `tadmor`) identifies the unit across all operations. Each [Nameplate](#Nameplate) declares its [Vessel](#Vessel) selections, [Hallmark](#Hallmark) pins, and the network policy that the [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces. ### Sentry Security container enforcing network policies via `iptables` and `dnsmasq`. The [Sentry](#Sentry) applies two layers of egress policy: DNS-level filtering (only allowed domain names resolve) and IP-level filtering (only allowed CIDR ranges pass). A compromised [Bottle](#Bottle) cannot bypass either layer — the [Sentry](#Sentry) is the sole gateway between the [Bottle](#Bottle) and the outside network. ### Pentacle Privileged container establishing the network namespace shared with the [Bottle](#Bottle). The [Pentacle](#Pentacle) runs briefly with elevated privileges to create the network topology, then remains as the namespace anchor. Security policies are enforced from the first packet because the [Sentry](#Sentry) configures the namespace before the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts. ### Bottle Your workload container, running unmodified in a controlled network environment. The [Bottle](#Bottle) has no direct network access — all traffic routes through the [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway in a namespace prepared by the [Pentacle](#Pentacle). Any existing container image can run as a [Bottle](#Bottle) without modification. ### Crucible The [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad running together as one unit for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). The [Crucible](#Crucible) is the local safety orchestration — the apparatus that makes running untrusted code practical. [Charging](#Charge) starts all three containers; [Quenching](#Quench) stops and cleans them up. ### Charge Start the [Sentry](#Sentry)/[Pentacle](#Pentacle)/[Bottle](#Bottle) triad for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). [Charging](#Charge) brings up the [Crucible](#Crucible) in dependency order: [Pentacle](#Pentacle) creates the namespace, [Sentry](#Sentry) configures policy, then the [Bottle](#Bottle) starts with its network already constrained. ### Quench Stop and clean up a [Charged](#Charge) [Nameplate's](#Nameplate) containers. [Quenching](#Quench) tears down the [Crucible](#Crucible) in reverse order and removes the network resources created during [Charging](#Charge). ## How It Works Recipe Bottle addresses two orthogonal complexity domains: building container images with verifiable provenance, and running untrusted images with enforced network isolation. The two tracks compose but neither requires the other. ### Image Management Recipe Bottle builds container images on Google Cloud Build (GCB) and stores them in Google Artifact Registry (GAR): - Isolated build environments using Google-curated Cloud Build builder images - Multi-architecture support via `docker buildx` with binfmt emulation - SLSA provenance attestation and verification - Software Bills of Material (SBOM) for every build - Full build transcripts captured as auxiliary metadata artifacts - Upstream base images [Enshrined](#Enshrine) into the [Depot's](#Depot) registry, so builds do not depend on third-party registry availability at build time - `gcloud` never runs on the workstation — REST calls via `curl` and `jq` drive all remote operations, and the Google-supplied `gcloud` binary is confined to Cloud Build step containers on the server side ### Crucible Orchestration For running containers with network services, Recipe Bottle orchestrates three containers working together: - **[Sentry](#Sentry)** — enforces network security policies via `iptables` and `dnsmasq` - **[Pentacle](#Pentacle)** — establishes a privileged network namespace and shares it with the [Bottle](#Bottle) - **[Bottle](#Bottle)** — your workload container, running unmodified in a controlled network environment This ensures security policies are enforced from the first packet, and the [Bottle](#Bottle) container experiences only a functional path to its [Sentry](#Sentry) gateway. The [Sentry](#Sentry) applies two layers of egress policy: `dnsmasq` answers DNS queries only for explicitly allowed names, and `iptables` permits outbound IP traffic only to allowed CIDR ranges. The two layers combine into an enforced allowlist that a compromised or misbehaving [Bottle](#Bottle) cannot bypass — neither by DNS-based exfiltration nor by direct IP connection to an unapproved destination. Each [Crucible's](#Crucible) allowlist is declared in a [Nameplate](#Nameplate) regime file alongside the [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) [Vessel](#Vessel) selections, making network policy a reviewable artifact of the configuration rather than an implicit runtime behavior. ### Project Direction **Build pipeline.** Egress lockdown is implemented via a dual-pool Cloud Build architecture. VPC Service Controls and cosign signing are evaluated and deferred until organizational policy or external distribution triggers them. **Runtime.** Docker on Linux is the first-class runtime; rootless Podman and macOS-native workflows are deferred pending user demand. One known weakness: when allowed domains are CDN-hosted (e.g. Cloudflare), the [Sentry's](#Sentry) CIDR allowlist becomes coarse — DNS-level gating remains precise, but IP-level gating is porous across shared CDN ranges. **Networking.** [Bottle](#Bottle)-to-[Bottle](#Bottle) communication is feasible under the current [Sentry](#Sentry) model but not implemented; waiting for a concrete use case. ## Prerequisites - macOS or Linux workstation - `bash` (3.2+), `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq`, `openssl` - `docker` (container runtime) - A Google Cloud account with billing enabled (credit card required for verification; free tier is sufficient to start) ## Using the CLI All Recipe Bottle operations are invoked through lightweight shell scripts in the `tt/` directory, run from the project root. Tab completion narrows by prefix. The project's `CLAUDE.md` provides a complete command reference; the CLI's interactive walkthroughs are the authoritative procedure source. ## Setup Recipe Bottle uses a role-based security model with four roles, each building on the previous: | Role | Authenticates via | Purpose | |------|-------------------|---------| | [**Payor**](#Payor) | OAuth (browser flow) | Creates/funds GCP infrastructure, manages [Governor](#Governor) lifecycle | | [**Governor**](#Governor) | Service account credential | Administers [Director](#Director) and [Retriever](#Retriever) credentials within a [Depot](#Depot) | | [**Director**](#Director) | Service account credential | Submits builds, manages images, verifies provenance | | [**Retriever**](#Retriever) | Service account credential | Pulls images for local use | The [Payor](#Payor) stands apart — it requires manual Google Cloud Console work and OAuth authentication. All downstream roles authenticate via credential files, enabling full automation. ### Establishment and Provisioning The [Payor](#Payor) begins by creating a GCP project and configuring an OAuth consent screen through the Google Cloud Console. After downloading the OAuth client credentials, the [Payor](#Payor) installs them via a browser authorization flow — the resulting refresh token is stored locally with restrictive permissions and does not need to be repeated. With [Payor](#Payor) credentials in place, the [Payor](#Payor) [Levies](#Levy) a [Depot](#Depot), provisioning it with build infrastructure, artifact registry, and secrets storage. The [Payor](#Payor) then mantles a [Governor](#Governor) service account to administer the [Depot](#Depot). ### Credential Distribution The [Governor](#Governor) creates downstream credentials: [Knighting](#Knight) a [Director](#Director) for build operations and [Chartering](#Charter) a [Retriever](#Retriever) for image pull access. Each credential is scoped to a single role within a single [Depot](#Depot). ### Build and Retrieve The [Director](#Director) [Ordains](#Ordain) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) for each [Vessel](#Vessel) — [Conjuring](#Conjure) from source, [Binding](#Bind) from upstream, or [Grafting](#Graft) from local builds. After builds complete, the [Director](#Director) [Tallies](#Tally) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) by health status and [Vouches](#Vouch) their provenance. [Hallmark](#Hallmark) values from the [Tally](#Tally) are recorded into [Nameplate](#Nameplate) regime files, completing the chain from build to runtime. The [Retriever](#Retriever) [Summons](#Summon) [Vouched](#Vouch) images locally for use. ## Day-to-Day Operations To run a workload, [Charge](#Charge) the [Crucible](#Crucible) for a [Nameplate](#Nameplate). This starts the [Sentry](#Sentry), [Pentacle](#Pentacle), and [Bottle](#Bottle) containers together — the [Bottle](#Bottle) is ready for interactive use immediately. For diagnostics, shell into the [Bottle](#Bottle) or the [Sentry](#Sentry), or observe network traffic across the [Crucible's](#Crucible) containers. When finished, [Quench](#Quench) the [Crucible](#Crucible) to stop and clean up all three containers. To inspect an image's supply chain, [Plumb](#Plumb) its provenance — the full view shows the SBOM, build info, and Dockerfile; the compact view summarizes the attestation chain. ## Credential Safety All credential files require `600` permissions and must never be committed to version control. | Credential | Location | Created during | |------------|----------|----------------| | [Payor](#Payor) OAuth | `~/.rbw/rbro.env` | [Payor](#Payor) installation | | [Governor](#Governor) | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/governor/rbra.env` | [Governor](#Governor) mantling | | [Director](#Director) | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/director/rbra.env` | [Director](#Director) [Knighting](#Knight) | | [Retriever](#Retriever) | `RBRR_SECRETS_DIR/retriever/rbra.env` | [Retriever](#Retriever) [Chartering](#Charter) | Each credential file is scoped to one role within one [Depot](#Depot) and cannot operate outside its designation. ## Configuration Recipe Bottle uses a Config Regime system — structured configuration with typed validation. Each regime has a render command (display current values) and a validate command (check correctness). ### User-Configured Regimes | Regime | File | Purpose | |--------|------|---------| | RBRP | `.rbk/rbrp.env` | [Payor](#Payor) GCP project identity | | RBRR | `.rbk/rbrr.env` | [Depot](#Depot) project, region, build config | | RBRN | `.rbk/*/rbrn.env` | Per-[Vessel](#Vessel): runtime, [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) | | RBRV | [Vessel](#Vessel) dirs | Container image build definitions | | RBRS | station file | Developer machine paths (not in git) | ### Managed Regimes (generated by operations) | Regime | Purpose | Generated during | |--------|---------|-----------------| | RBRO | OAuth refresh token | [Payor](#Payor) installation | | RBRA | Service account credentials | Mantling, [Knighting](#Knight), [Chartering](#Charter) | ### BUK Base Regimes | Regime | File | Purpose | |--------|------|---------| | BURC | `.buk/burc.env` | Project structure (tabtarget dir, tools dir) | | BURS | `../station-files/burs.env` | Developer machine (log dir). Not in git. | ## Vessels and Nameplates **[Vessels](#Vessel)** are build definitions — each is a directory under `rbev-vessels/`: ``` rbev-vessels/ ├── rbev-sentry-debian-slim/ # Sentry vessel (conjure — built from source) │ ├── Dockerfile │ └── rbrv.env ├── rbev-bottle-ifrit/ # Ifrit bottle vessel (conjure — built from source) │ ├── Dockerfile │ └── rbrv.env └── rbev-bottle-plantuml/ # PlantUML server (bind — upstream image pinned by digest) └── rbrv.env ``` [Conjure](#Conjure) [Vessels](#Vessel) have a Dockerfile and are built by Cloud Build. [Bind](#Bind) [Vessels](#Vessel) (like `rbev-bottle-plantuml`) pin an external image by digest in `rbrv.env` — no Dockerfile, no build step. [Graft](#Graft) [Vessels](#Vessel) push a locally-built image to GAR via docker push — no Cloud Build for the image, but about and [Vouch](#Vouch) still run in Cloud Build. The same [Ordain](#Ordain) operation handles all three: it detects the [Vessel](#Vessel) mode and triggers a Cloud Build ([Conjure](#Conjure)), mirrors from upstream ([Bind](#Bind)), or pushes a local image ([Graft](#Graft)). Trust hierarchy: [Conjure](#Conjure) has full SLSA provenance, [Bind](#Bind) has digest-pin verification, [Graft](#Graft) has no provenance chain (GRAFTED verdict). **[Nameplates](#Nameplate)** tie [Vessels](#Vessel) together into a runnable [Crucible](#Crucible). The [Nameplate](#Nameplate) moniker (e.g. `tadmor`) identifies the unit across all operations: ``` .rbk/tadmor/rbrn.env # Maps tadmor → rbev-sentry-debian-slim + rbev-bottle-ifrit ``` [Charging](#Charge) `tadmor` starts the [Crucible](#Crucible) defined by the `tadmor` [Nameplate](#Nameplate), which selects its [Sentry](#Sentry) and [Bottle](#Bottle) [Vessel](#Vessel) images. ## Testing Run test fixtures sequentially — they share regime state and container namespaces. Never run fixtures in parallel. Two qualification gates exercise the full system: a fast qualify checks tabtarget structure, colophon integrity, and [Nameplate](#Nameplate) health; a release qualify adds shellcheck analysis and the complete test suite. ## Recovery - **Lost OAuth credentials**: Download a fresh JSON key from Google Cloud Console and re-run the [Payor](#Payor) installation - **Expired tokens**: Run the [Payor](#Payor) refresh operation - **Compromised [Governor](#Governor)**: Re-mantle the [Governor](#Governor) (replaces the service account, invalidates the old credential) - **Compromised [Director](#Director)/[Retriever](#Retriever)**: Forfeit the compromised account, then re-[Knight](#Knight) or re-[Charter](#Charter) - **Lost [Nameplate](#Nameplate) values**: Re-[Tally](#Tally) [Hallmarks](#Hallmark) to retrieve values from the registry - **Build timeout or failure**: [Tally](#Tally) to check build status, review logs in the GCP Console for the [Depot](#Depot) project ## Architecture ``` Project Root/ ├── .buk/ # BUK launcher directory + BURC config ├── .rbk/ # Recipe Bottle config regimes ├── tt/ # TabTargets — all CLI commands live here ├── Tools/ │ ├── buk/ # Bash Utility Kit (portable CLI infrastructure) │ └── rbk/ # Recipe Bottle Kit (domain logic) └── rbev-vessels/ # Vessel definitions (Dockerfile + rbrv.env per vessel) ``` ## Claude Code If you use [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code), the project includes a `CLAUDE.md` with a full command reference table, glossary, and conventions for AI-assisted development. ## License Copyright 2026 Scale Invariant, Inc. Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0.

content.text:
Here's my analysis of the current blockquote and what's weak:

**Crucible problems:**
- "three-container security apparatus where a privileged Pentacle establishes the network namespace, a Sentry enforces iptables policy on dual networks" — this teaches plumbing before the reader knows what any of these words mean. At blockquote level, the reader needs security *properties*, not component names.
- "dual networks" is vague — the real story is dual-layer enforcement (DNS + IP).
- Pentacle doesn't earn its mention here; it's an implementation detail at this altitude.

**Supply chain gap** (your point):
- Current text says "SLSA provenance verification, least-privilege service accounts, and no secrets in version control" — all defensive posture, but misses the headline: egress-locked builds on Cloud Build, digest-pinned toolchains, mirrored base images. The airgap is the distinctive claim.

Two proposals — both restructure as domain-parallel sentences:

**Option A — tight, three sentences:**
> Recipe Bottle builds container images in egress-locked ("airgap") Cloud Build with SLSA provenance and digest-pinned toolchains. It runs untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) — a three-container apparatus where a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces dual-layer egress policy (DNS filtering + IP filtering) and the [Bottle](#Bottle) workload runs unmodified with no direct network access. Credentials use least-privilege scoping; no secrets are stored in version control.

**Option B — fuller, still two sentences:**
> Recipe Bottle builds container images in an egress-locked Cloud Build environment with SLSA provenance attestation, digest-pinned toolchains, and upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned registry. It runs untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) where a dedicated [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces dual-layer egress policy — DNS filtering via `dnsmasq` and IP filtering via `iptables` — while the [Bottle](#Bottle) workload has no direct network access and runs unmodified.

Option A is punchier. Option B names more mechanisms (dnsmasq/iptables, mirrored base images) which fits the security-review audience who wants to know *what* to audit. Both drop Pentacle from this altitude.

The second paragraph ("This architecture is deliberate...") reads well either way. Preference?

message.content: I lean towards option A, but its oversimplistic about the 'supply chain' permutations intended by recipe bottle: I want the range characterized.  its not all air grap.  its more than digest-pinned tool chains: also local base image caches.  try again.  Hmm, instead of 'workload runs unmodified', consider: "Bottle container which is unprivileged in Pentacle net namespace and thus interacts through Sentry's network..." Hmm, see if you can work that in better.

content.text:
Good feedback. The supply chain isn't one thing — it's three modes with a shared substrate. And the Bottle's position in Pentacle's namespace is *why* the Sentry can't be bypassed — that's a security property worth stating.

**Revised proposal:**

> Recipe Bottle's supply chain spans three modes — egress-locked Cloud Build from source with SLSA provenance, digest-pinned mirrors of upstream images, and local pushes — all drawing from base images mirrored into a project-owned registry with digest-pinned toolchains. It runs untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) — the [Bottle](#Bottle) runs unprivileged in a network namespace created by the [Pentacle](#Pentacle), so all traffic necessarily flows through a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforcing dual-layer egress policy (DNS filtering + IP filtering). Credentials use least-privilege scoping; no secrets are stored in version control.

The crucible sentence now earns Pentacle — it explains the structural guarantee (namespace isolation → traffic must flow through Sentry) rather than just listing components. "Necessarily" does real work there.

The supply chain sentence names the three modes, then the shared substrate (mirrored base images, digest-pinned toolchains). The reader sees both the range and the common hardening.

message.content:
I'm musing the below line should be placed before the "Important" section and smoothed:

Recipe Bottle helps you build container images with rigorous supply-chain provenance, and run untrusted containers inside a Crucible that enforces network isolation without touching the workload. A developer can adopt either domain alone; the two are designed to compose, not to depend on each other.

... Can you you work on improving that first sentence clause to characterize the full supply chain capability?  Just try, I'm not sure how to do this myself.  Then we can simplify the "Important" text to not duplicate that intro paragraph.

content.text:
A few attempts at the elevator pitch with the supply chain range characterized:

**A — em-dash parenthetical for the modes:**
> Recipe Bottle helps you produce container images across three supply-chain modes — egress-locked Cloud Build from source, digest-pinned upstream mirrors, and local pushes — and run untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) that enforces network isolation without touching the workload.

**B — leading with the airgap as headline, range as follow-on:**
> Recipe Bottle helps you build container images with egress-locked Cloud Build and verifiable provenance — supporting source builds, digest-pinned upstream mirrors, and local pushes — and run untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) that enforces network isolation without touching the workload.

**C — tighter, trades enumeration for a characterizing phrase:**
> Recipe Bottle helps you build container images with supply-chain provenance across a spectrum from egress-locked Cloud Build to digest-pinned upstream mirrors, and run untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) that enforces network isolation without touching the workload.

A is most explicit — names all three modes. B leads with the headline feature (airgap) and tucks the range behind it. C is shortest but drops "local pushes" to stay compact.

If the elevator pitch carries this weight, the Important blockquote can shed its first-encounter explanatory burden and focus purely on "here's our security posture, here's what needs review" — more like the revised three-sentence version we just discussed.

message.content: I choose A.  Implement A and then also attempt the "improvement" cleanup, then notch and push.

1 # Recipe Bottle 2 3 > [!IMPORTANT] 4 > **Early-stage project — security review welcome** 5 > 6 > Recipe Bottle runs untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) — a three-container security apparatus where a privileged [Pentacle](#Pentacle) establishes the network namespace, a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces iptables policy on dual networks, and the [Bottle](#Bottle) workload has no direct network access. The supply chain is hardened with SLSA provenance verification, least-privilege service accounts, and no secrets in version control. 7 > 8 > This architecture is deliberate, but it has not yet had broad independent review — particularly the runtime containment (iptables rules, privileged namespace setup, network isolation enforcement). If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. 9 10 Recipe Bottle helps you build container images with rigorous supply-chain provenance, and run untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) that enforces network isolation without touching the workload. A developer can adopt either domain alone; the two are designed to compose, not to depend on each other. 11 12 Recipe Bottle is a set of bash scripts designed to be incorporated into arbitrary projects — via `git subtree`, `git subrepo`, `git submodule`, or simply by copying a few directories into place. The workstation floor is deliberately narrow: `bash 3.2`, `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq`, `openssl`, and `docker`. There is no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, no `gcloud` CLI on the workstation — Google Cloud operations run as REST calls over `curl` and `jq`. A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. 13 14 **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle 15 16

17 18

19 20 ## Supply Chain 21 22 Recipe Bottle orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with SLSA attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. Builds run in an egress-locked configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability.

# Recipe Bottle > [!IMPORTANT] > **Early-stage project — security review welcome** > > Recipe Bottle runs untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) — a three-container security apparatus where a privileged [Pentacle](#Pentacle) establishes the network namespace, a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforces iptables policy on dual networks, and the [Bottle](#Bottle) workload has no direct network access. The supply chain is hardened with SLSA provenance verification, least-privilege service accounts, and no secrets in version control. > > This architecture is deliberate, but it has not yet had broad independent review — particularly the runtime containment (iptables rules, privileged namespace setup, network isolation enforcement). If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued. Recipe Bottle helps you build container images with rigorous supply-chain provenance, and run untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) that enforces network isolation without touching the workload. A developer can adopt either domain alone; the two are designed to compose, not to depend on each other. Recipe Bottle is a set of bash scripts designed to be incorporated into arbitrary projects — via `git subtree`, `git subrepo`, `git submodule`, or simply by copying a few directories into place. The workstation floor is deliberately narrow: `bash 3.2`, `git`, `curl`, `openssh`, `jq`, `openssl`, and `docker`. There is no Python runtime, no language-specific package manager, no `gcloud` CLI on the workstation — Google Cloud operations run as REST calls over `curl` and `jq`. A small team can stand up a hardened build pipeline and a sandboxed runtime without specialized DevOps expertise. **Project page**: https://scaleinv.github.io/recipebottle

## Supply Chain Recipe Bottle orchestrates Google Cloud Build to produce container images with SLSA attestation, software bills of material, reproducible multi-architecture builds, and digest-pinned toolchains — so every image has a verifiable origin story. Builds run in an egress-locked configuration, drawing from upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned registry — a fixed, self-contained supply chain independent of third-party registry availability.

input.new_string:
# Recipe Bottle

Recipe Bottle helps you produce container images across three supply-chain modes — egress-locked Cloud Build from source, digest-pinned upstream mirrors, and local pushes — and run untrusted containers inside a [Crucible](#Crucible) that enforces network isolation without touching the workload. A developer can adopt either domain alone; the two are designed to compose, not to depend on each other.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Early-stage project — security review welcome**
>
> Builds use SLSA provenance attestation, digest-pinned toolchains, and upstream base images mirrored into a project-owned registry. The [Bottle](#Bottle) runs unprivileged in a network namespace created by the [Pentacle](#Pentacle), so all traffic necessarily flows through a [Sentry](#Sentry) enforcing dual-layer egress policy (DNS filtering + IP filtering). Credentials use least-privilege scoping; no secrets are stored in version control.
>
> This architecture is deliberate, but it has not yet had broad independent review — particularly the runtime containment (iptables rules, privileged namespace setup, network isolation enforcement). If you evaluate or deploy this, you are contributing to its hardening. Security-focused contributors and responsible disclosure are especially valued.
